annotations.text: One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
Sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is a frequent finding in this uncommon disorder and can be refractory to treatment.
The tachycardia usually has a left-bundle type of configuration.
In this case ventricular tachycardia was occurring in the setting of a global cardiomyopathy or myocarditis.
Cardiomyopathies comprise dilated, hypertrophic, and restrictive types.

The least common is restrictive cardiomyopathy, which is usually caused by an infiltrative disease, such as amyloidosis, hemochromatosis, glycogen-storage disorders, fibroplastic disorders, eosinophilic disorders, and neoplastic disease.
The characteristic echocardiographic findings include increased wall thickness without chamber enlargement and evidence of abnormal diastolic function with ultimate systolic dysfunction.
Neoplasms can involve the heart directly or, more commonly, through metastasis, although less than 10 percent of patients with cardiac tumors have clinically overt cardiac disease.
The most common type of involvement is pericarditis, which usually results from metastatic breast or lung cancer.
In view of the presence of thoracic lymphadenopathy in this young nonsmoker, the possibility of lymphoma should be considered.
Cardiac involvement, which is found at autopsy in up to 24 percent of patients with lymphoma, is typically seen in the form of extension to the parietal pericardium, with or without effusions.
A presentation with overt heart disease is rare and generally followed by a fulminant course because of the association with high-grade tumors.
The diagnosis of sarcoidosis should be strongly considered in any young patient with conduction-system disease and ventricular tachycardia.
Sarcoidosis is a systemic granulomatous disorder of unknown cause characterized by noncaseating epithelioid-cell granulomas involving multiple organ systems.
The most frequent presentation of sarcoidosis is in young adults with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates, and skin or eye lesions.
Generalized cutaneous anergy occurs in more than two thirds of the patients.
Granulomatous involvement of the heart can cause either a restrictive or a dilated cardiomyopathy.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Although 20 to 25 percent of patients with sarcoidosis have cardiac involvement at autopsy, only 5 percent of them have clinically overt disease.
Most of the patients with confirmed cardiac involvement die suddenly.

The presentation may also include myocarditis with congestive heart failure, sudden cardiac death, supraventricular tachyarrhythmia, mitral-valve insufficiency due to papillary-muscle dysfunction, formation of a left ventricular aneurysm, acute myocardial infarction, and pericarditis.
I believe that this patient had systemic sarcoidosis involving the heart and lungs.
Generalized cutaneous anergy is typical of sarcoidosis.
the cardiomyopathy, conduction-system dysfunction, and sustained ventricular tachycardia are common manifestations of cardiac sarcoidosis.
